title: PAS git deps pinned to `main` branch — non-reproducible
description: Two Move git deps (`pas`, `ptb`) declared with `rev = "main"`. Future audits cannot recover the exact build without the lockfile; a clean clone will pull current main.
impact: Audit reproducibility. An upstream change could silently alter Hadron's compliance semantics (policy behavior, request resolution, template storage).
recommendation: Pin to the commit SHA already in Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) and treat rev bumps as reviewed changes.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: Adversarial: is there a way to bypass the transfer whitelist via a crafted Request?
description: Walkthrough: a caller needs a `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>`. The only way to construct one is via `pas::account::send_balance<C>` (account.move L78) which takes `&Auth` from the account owner and returns the Request. Attempting to forge sender/recipient addresses requires owning the source account (auth). Therefore the sender field is trustworthy. Recipient is supplied by the caller to send_balance — a malicious caller could set `to = some_account` but the upstream `to.owner()` is derived from the destination Account at send-balance time; recipient address = to.owner(). So the recipient in the Request is the actual destination account's owner.
impact: No bypass path found. The Request's sender/recipient are upstream-validated.
recommendation: Record this walkthrough as validated. Add a comment in `approve_transfer` citing the send_balance upstream path for future auditors.

title: Adversarial: can a request be approved twice?
description: Upstream `pas::request::Request::approve<K, U>` uses `VecSet.insert` which ABORTS on duplicate. So a second `request.approve(TransferApproval<T>())` on the same request would abort. A malicious caller attempting double-approval to confuse the state fails safely.

title: Adversarial: can a caller bypass the whitelist by using a forged Treasury reference?
description: The Treasury is a shared object; callers supply `&Treasury` via `tx.object(treasuryId)`. They cannot forge an alternate treasury because the consolidator-verified `approve_transfer` function signature requires any Treasury (only `&Treasury`). A malicious caller could supply a DIFFERENT Treasury (not the one that owns T) — but the `can_send<T>` / `can_receive<T>` checks would query the wrong treasury's whitelist DOF, and either (a) return false (address not in that treasury's whitelist for T) and abort, or (b) if the wrong treasury happens to have T registered too, succeed — but T is uniquely bound to one treasury per OTW uniqueness, so this isn't actually reachable on-chain.
impact: Interesting theoretical: if two treasuries could both register the same T (impossible via OTW uniqueness), an attacker could forum-shop treasuries. In practice, TreasuryCap<T> is created once per package and consumed once, so this scenario is unreachable.
recommendation: Document the OTW-uniqueness invariant is load-bearing here. If Hadron ever adds a mechanism to 'import' caps from external coins (without OTW guarantee), this invariant breaks.

title: set_whitelist_send/receive does not re-validate that the address is still in the whitelist atomically with the update
description: `set_whitelist_send<T>` and `set_whitelist_receive<T>` assert `wl.contains(addr)` then mutate `wl[addr].can_send`/`can_receive`. Since these functions take `&mut Treasury`, no concurrent modification can happen within the same transaction — the check-then-set is atomic. Worth confirming.
impact: None — Move transaction atomicity ensures no TOCTOU here.
recommendation: No action. Documented for audit record.

title: BUG: set_whitelist_send actually writes to can_RECEIVE (copy-paste error suspected)
description: Reading the function body carefully: `set_whitelist_send<T>` assigns `wl[addr].can_send = enabled;` — this is CORRECT. (Retracting from initial suspicion; the code is correct.) HOWEVER, the symmetric review of `set_whitelist_receive<T>` also shows `wl[addr].can_receive = enabled;` — correct. The evidence block below is cited as false alarm during my review.
impact: None — after re-read, both functions write to the correct field. Withdrawn as not-a-bug.
recommendation: Withdraw. Included in JSON per schema (every finding must have all fields); consolidator should reject this as a false positive during verification.

title: whitelist functions on non-PAS coin abort via framework — add explicit ENotPasCoin guard
description: All whitelist helpers borrow the whitelist DOF without first checking `is_pas_coin<T>`. For non-PAS T, the abort happens inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow{_mut}` with a framework-level error, not the Hadron-defined `ENotPasCoin`.
impact: Error UX. No bypass. Callers of `is_whitelisted<T>(treasury, addr)` on a non-PAS T abort the PTB.
recommendation: Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` as the first line of each of `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `assert_can_send`, `assert_can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`.

title: pas_admin.move imports pas::keys but not hadron::pas_transfer — indirect coupling
description: `pas_admin` uses `hadron::pas_transfer::transfer_approval_permit<T>()` inside `register_transfer_template`. The use is correct (needs the permit witness), but creates a one-way dependency from pas_admin → pas_transfer. pas_transfer has no dependency on pas_admin. Clean dependency graph.

title: PAS accounts are created permissionlessly (upstream) — any address can have an account
description: Upstream `pas::account::create` (account.move L42) is permissionless. Anyone can create a PAS account for any address. Hadron's `pas_supply::mint<T>` asserts `account.owner()` is whitelisted before depositing — so minting into an unsanctioned account aborts. Good gating.

title: Move.toml edition is 2024.beta (not 2024)
description: Edition value `2024.beta` is the preview-period identifier. Should be `2024` for the released edition.
impact: Low. Toolchain may deprecate beta identifier.

title: token_template_pas/Move.toml edition and deps — confirm match
description: The new `token_template_pas` package has its own Move.toml. Confirm edition value and that deps list is minimal (only Sui framework, no PAS needed since the template itself doesn't reference PAS).
recommendation: Check manually and update to edition = "2024" if still beta. Keep deps minimal.

title: register_pas_asset does not emit a `PasPolicyConfigured<T>` event — only PasAssetRegistered
description: The function sets 2 required approvals (TransferApproval, ClawbackApproval), shares the policy, adds caps, AND registers a template — but emits only one event (`PasAssetRegistered<T>`, emitted inside `add_pas_coin_caps`). Policy sharing, template registration, and required-approval settings produce no distinct audit trail.
impact: Indexer cannot reconstruct the policy configuration from events alone; must query on-chain state.
recommendation: Either: (a) add events for each policy-shape change (`PasPolicyCreated`, `RequiredApprovalSet`, `TransferTemplateRegistered`), or (b) expand `PasAssetRegistered` to carry policy_id, required_approvals, template_keyed_by. Option (b) is cheaper.

title: No test coverage for PAS paths — critical gap
description: Zero `#[test]` functions for any new PAS module. The three new modules (pas_admin, pas_supply, pas_transfer) and the PAS extensions to treasury.move have no automated tests.
impact: Regressions in whitelist gating, witness wiring, seize asymmetry, burn flow, and template registration can ship to mainnet undetected.
recommendation: Prioritize: (1) `register_pas_asset` happy + duplicate abort; (2) `add_to_whitelist` / `remove_from_whitelist` / `set_whitelist_*`; (3) `mint` with non-whitelisted recipient aborts; (4) `burn` via clawback flow; (5) `seize` source-not-whitelisted + dest-whitelisted path; (6) `approve_transfer` negative matrix (amount=0, paused, sender/receiver not whitelisted, stale version). Use `/move-tests` to scaffold.

title: scripts/src/operations/pas.ts has no function to seize — on-chain seize exposed, off-chain script not
description: The TypeScript operations expose register, whitelist, mint — but not seize or burn. On-chain `pas_supply::seize` and `pas_supply::burn` are available but have no client-side helper. Operators must hand-craft the PTB.
impact: Operational friction. Not a safety issue.
recommendation: Add `seizePasTokens` and `burnPasTokens` TS helpers that construct the prerequisite clawback request via `pasClient.call.clawbackBalance` and then call the hadron approve.
evidence: export { registerPasAsset, deployAndRegisterPasAsset, addToWhitelist, removeFromWhitelist, mintPasTokens } from './pas';

title: Missing setWhitelistSend / setWhitelistReceive TS helpers
description: `addToWhitelist` and `removeFromWhitelist` are exposed, but `set_whitelist_send` and `set_whitelist_receive` (on-chain) have no corresponding TS wrapper. An operator wanting to toggle send-only / receive-only for an address must hand-craft.
recommendation: Add `setWhitelistSend(treasuryId, coinType, addr, enabled)` and `setWhitelistReceive(...)` TS helpers.

title: events.move `emit_pas_transfer_approved` called only from pas_transfer — cross-module emit ok
description: The emit helpers are `public(package)`, so only in-package modules can emit. pas_transfer and pas_supply both emit — consistent with phase-1 pattern.
